concepts.title: First Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.conceptBody:
First Law of Thermodynamics

First law is a relation among heat, work and internal energy.

Let a system changes from an initial equilibrium state $i$ to a final equilibrium state $\text{f}$ in a definite way, the heat absorbed by the system being $\text{Q}$ and the word done by the system being $\text{W}$. Then we compute the $\text{Q}-\text{W}$. While $\text{Q}$ and $\text{W}$ both depend on the thermodynamic path taken between two equilibrium states, their difference $\text{Q}-\text{W}$ does not. We do this over and over again, using different paths each time. We find that in every case the quantity $Q-W$ is the same. In thermodynamics there is a function of the thermodynamic coordinates $(\text{P},\text{V}$ and $\text{T})$ whose final value minus its initial value equals the change $Q-W$ in the process. We call this function the internal energy function. We have,

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}={{\text{U}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{U}}_{\text{i}}}=\text{Q}-\text{W}$ (1)

This equation is known as the first law of thermodynamics.

We describe the energy relations in any thermodynamics process in terms of the quantity of heat $\text{Q}$ added to the system and the work $\text{W}$ done by the system. Both $\text{Q}$ and $\text{W}$ may be positive, negative or zero. A positive value of $\text{Q}$ represents heat flow heat flow into the system, negative $\text{Q}$ represents heat flow out of the system. A positive value of $\text{W}$ represents work done by the system against its surroundings, such as work done by an expanding gas. Negative W represents work done on the system by its surroundings such as work done during compression of a gas.

concepts.title: Heat
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{Q} \right)$ 

It is the energy that is transferred between a system and its environment because of temperature difference between them.

In case of solids and liquids,

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}=\text{mL}$ if there is change of state

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}=\text{mC }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ T}$ if there is change in temperature

In case of gases,

$\text{Q}=\text{nC }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ T}$

in differential form $\text{dQ}=\text{nCdT}$

where $\text{C}$ is the molar heat capacity of the gas and $\text{n}$ is the number of moles of the gas.

and always take, $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ T}={{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{T}}_{\text{i}}}$

where ${{T}_{f}}$ is the final temperature and ${{T}_{i}}$ is the initial temperature of gas. Also, the molar heat capacity of an ideal gas

$\text{C}=\frac{\text{R}}{\gamma -1}+\frac{\text{R}}{1-\text{x}}={{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}+\frac{\text{R}}{1-\text{x}}$

$\text{C}={{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}=\frac{\text{R}}{\gamma -1}$ in isochoric process and

$\text{C}={{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}={{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}+\text{R}$ in isobaric process.

Note : 

(1) SI unit of heat is Joule and CGS is calorie [1 cal = 4.2 J]

(2) It is path dependent, e.g., heat required to change the temperature of same gas through same amount at constant volume.

concepts.title: Work Done
concepts.conceptBody:
Work $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{W} \right)$ 

Work can be defined as the energy that is transferred from one body to other owing to a force that acts between them. The amount of work done by a system as it expands or contracts is given by

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ W}=\int ~\text{dW}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{v}}_{\text{i}}}}^{{{\text{v}}_{\text{f}}}}\text{PdV}$

Derivation 

Figure shows a gas confined to a cylinder that has a movable piston at one end. If the gas expands against the piston, it exerts a force through a distance and does work on the piston. If the piston compression the gas as it is moved inward, work is also done-in this case on the gas. The work associated with such volume changes can be determined as follows.

Let the gas pressure on the piston face be $\text{P}$. Then the force on the piston due to gas is PA, where $A$ is the area of the face.

When the piston is pushed outward an infinitesimal distance $\text{dx}$, the word done by the gas is

$\text{dW}=\text{F}\cdot \text{dx}=\text{PAdx}$

which, since the change in volume of the gas is $dV=Adx$, becomes

$\text{dW}=\text{PdV}$

For a finite change in volume from ${{V}_{i}}$ to ${{V}_{f}}$, this equation is then integrated between ${{V}_{i}}$ to ${{V}_{f}}$ to find the net work

$\text{W}=\int ~\text{dW}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}}^{{{\text{V}}_{\text{f}}}}\text{PdV}$

Note : 

(1) Like heat, work is also path dependent, scalar physical quantity.

(2) In a cyclic process work done is equal to the area under the cycle and is positive if the cycle is clockwise and negative if anticlockwise.

concepts.title: Internal Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Internal Energy $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U} \right)$

Internal energy of a system is the energy possessed by the system due to molecular motion and molecular configuration.

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}=\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}\text{dT}$

This equation can be applied in any process, whether it is isobaric, isothermal, adiabatic or else.

Note :

(1) Change in internal energy is path independent and depends only on the initial and final states of the system.

(2) Change in internal energy in a cyclic process is always zero $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}={{\text{U}}_{\text{F}}}-{{\text{U}}_{\text{C}}}=0$

concepts.title: Sign Conventions
concepts.conceptBody:
Sign Conventions

Thermodynamic sign conventions for heat and work

concepts.title: First Law Derivation
concepts.conceptBody:
First Law Derivation

Equation (i) can be written as

$\text{Q}-{{\text{W}}_{1}}={{\text{Q}}_{2}}-{{\text{W}}_{2}}1$

or $\Delta{{\text{U}}_{1}}=\Delta{{\text{U}}_{2}}$

that is the change in the internal energy of the system between two points is path independent. It depends on thermodynamic coordinates of the two points. For example, in case of an ideal gas it depends only on the initial and final temperatures.

Often the first law must be used in its differential form, which is

$\text{d}U=\text{d}Q-\text{dW}$ (2)

This can also be written as

$\mathrm{dQ}=\mathrm{dU}+\mathrm{dW}$ (3)

0r $\mathrm{Q}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{W}$ (4)

Note : 

(1) For solids/liquids pressure is assumed to be constant through the process, $\Delta\text{ W}={{\text{P}}_{\text{atm }}}\Delta\text{ V}$

(2) For gases, we will be frequently using the following results for calculations.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Delta\text{ W} =\int \text{Pdv} \\ \text{PV} =\text{nRT} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Work Done by the Gas
concepts.conceptBody:
Work Done by the Gas

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{dW} & ~=\text{F}\cdot \text{dx} \\ \text{dW} & ~=\text{PA}d\text{x} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ W}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{v}}_{\text{i}}}}^{{{\text{v}}_{\text{f}}}}\text{PdV}$

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ W}=$ area under the $\text{P}-\text{V}$ curve. 
 

Work done in each case

= area under the P-V curve
